insert_with_null_and_defaults.label: Insert with NULL and Defaults
insert_with_null_and_defaults.summary: Learn how missing values can work during inserts, how default values behave, how optional columns differ from required ones, and what basic insert behavior looks like.

sketch0.title: What happens when a value is missing?

sketch1.title: What does a default value do?

sketch3.title: How do inserts behave with NULL and defaults?

quiz.title: Quiz: Insert with NULL and Defaults

missing_values_during_insert.title: Some inserts can leave certain columns unfilled on purpose.
missing_values_during_insert.bodyMarkdown:
Not every column always needs a value from the user.

If a column is optional, or if the schema provides a default, the insert can still work.

That means a missing value does not always mean an error.

So the big idea is:

> some columns can be left out during an insert, depending on the schema

default_values.title: A default value is what SQL uses when you do not supply one.
default_values.bodyMarkdown:
A **default** gives SQL a fallback value for a column.

That means if you leave that column out of the insert, SQL can fill it automatically.

In this dataset, examples include default-style values for:

- `status`
- `is_test`
- `last_updated`

So the key idea is:

> defaults let SQL fill certain columns automatically when you omit them

optional_columns.title: An optional column can accept NULL or be left out when the schema allows it.
optional_columns.bodyMarkdown:
An optional column is different from a required column.

A required column must receive a value somehow.
An optional column can allow a missing value.

In this dataset, `notes` is the beginner-friendly example of an optional column.

So the main idea is:

> optional columns give inserts more flexibility

insert_behavior_basics.title: Insert behavior depends on whether a column is required, optional, or has a default.
insert_behavior_basics.bodyMarkdown:
When a row is inserted, SQL checks the schema rules.

A column can behave in different ways:

- required and must be filled
- optional and can be NULL
- defaulted and can be omitted

So the important idea is:

> insert behavior makes more sense when you know which columns are required, optional, or defaulted

m11_s03_insert_with_null_notes.title: Insert with NULL
m11_s03_insert_with_null_notes.prompt:
The SQLite workspace already has the `inventory_ops` dataset loaded.

Write a query that inserts one new row into `inventory_items` with these values:

- `id` = 7
- `name` = 'Ceramic Tray'
- `category` = 'Decor'
- `price` = 18.50
- `status` = 'active'
- `is_test` = 0
- `notes` = NULL
- `last_updated` = '2026-02-07'

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: An optional column can sometimes be given NULL explicitly during an insert.
help.hint_1: Use a normal insert and place NULL in the notes position.
help.hint_2: Build the row with all listed columns present, and use NULL where the optional notes value should go.

m11_s03_insert_with_null_notes.hint: Use NULL for the optional notes column.
m11_s03_insert_with_null_notes.starterCode: -- Insert one row with NULL in notes

m11_s03_insert_using_defaults.title: Let defaults fill missing values
m11_s03_insert_using_defaults.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that inserts one new row into `inventory_items` using only these columns:

- `id` = 8
- `name` = 'Clip Board'
- `category` = 'Accessories'
- `price` = 6.00
- `notes` = 'Office prep'

Leave out `status`, `is_test`, and `last_updated` so their default values are used.

Then run it.

help.concept: If a column has a default, you can often omit it from the column list and let SQL fill it automatically.
help.hint_1: Only list the columns you are explicitly supplying.
help.hint_2: Build the insert with a shorter column list so the omitted defaulted columns can fill themselves automatically.

m11_s03_insert_using_defaults.hint: Only include the columns you want to supply directly.
m11_s03_insert_using_defaults.starterCode: -- Insert one row and let defaults fill omitted columns

m11_s03_insert_optional_column_omitted.title: Omit an optional column
m11_s03_insert_optional_column_omitted.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that inserts one new row into `inventory_items` with these values:

- `id` = 9
- `name` = 'Counter Mat'
- `category` = 'Kitchen'
- `price` = 14.25
- `status` = 'draft'
- `is_test` = 0
- `last_updated` = '2026-02-09'

Omit the optional `notes` column completely.

Then click **Run**.

help.concept: Optional columns can sometimes be left out completely if the schema allows it.
help.hint_1: Do not include `notes` in the column list for this insert.
help.hint_2: Build the insert with only the columns you are actually supplying, leaving the optional notes column out of both the column list and the values.

m11_s03_insert_optional_column_omitted.hint: Leave the optional notes column out of the column list entirely.
m11_s03_insert_optional_column_omitted.starterCode: -- Insert one row while omitting the optional notes column

m11_s03_read_defaulted_row.title: Read a row that used defaults
m11_s03_read_defaulted_row.prompt:
The tables already exist.

Write a query that returns all columns from `inventory_items` where `id` = 1.

Then run it and inspect how optional or default-style columns appear in a stored row.

help.concept: Reading stored rows helps learners see what NULL and defaulted values look like in the table.
help.hint_1: Use a normal SELECT query with a WHERE condition on id.
help.hint_2: Build a query that reads the full row for one item so you can inspect how the optional and default-capable columns look after storage.

m11_s03_read_defaulted_row.hint: Use SELECT * with a WHERE condition on id.
m11_s03_read_defaulted_row.starterCode: -- Show one full inventory row for inspection

m11_s03_null_defaults_project.title: Practice NULL and defaults
m11_s03_null_defaults_project.prompt:
Use the SQL workspace on the right to practice inserts with NULL and defaults on the `inventory_ops` dataset.

For each task:

1. write the insert carefully
2. decide which columns are required, optional, or defaulted
3. click **Run**
4. read the row afterward when needed

Tasks:

1. Insert one row with NULL in `notes`
2. Insert one row while letting defaults fill `status`, `is_test`, and `last_updated`
3. Insert one row while omitting the optional `notes` column
4. Read a row back and inspect the stored values
5. Insert one row with `notes` supplied explicitly and defaults used for the other omitted columns

Run one query at a time.
This project is about understanding how missing values behave during inserts.

help.concept: NULL/default practice becomes clearer when learners decide which columns must be filled directly and which can be omitted or left NULL.
help.hint_1: Start by separating required columns from optional or defaulted ones.
help.hint_2: Solve the tasks one by one. First decide which columns need values from you, then decide which columns can be omitted or filled with NULL.

m11_s03_null_defaults_project.hint: Decide which columns are required, optional, or defaulted before writing the insert.
m11_s03_null_defaults_project.starterCode:
-- Task 1
-- Insert one row with NULL in notes

m11_s03_missing_values_meaning.title: What can happen with missing values during insert?
m11_s03_missing_values_meaning.prompt: What is the main beginner idea about missing values during inserts?

help.concept: A missing value is not always an error if the schema allows omission or NULL.
help.hint_2: Whether a missing value is acceptable depends on the column rules.

m11_s03_missing_values_meaning.hint: Think a missing value is not always an error.

options.a: A missing value can be okay if the schema allows it
options.b: A missing value always crashes every insert
options.c: Missing values only exist in comments
options.d: Every column always requires a manual value

m11_s03_defaults_meaning.title: What does a default value do?
m11_s03_defaults_meaning.prompt: What is the main job of a default value in a table schema?

help.concept: A default gives SQL a value to use when the insert omits that column.
help.hint_2: If you leave the column out, SQL can fill it automatically.

options.a: It gives SQL a fallback value when that column is omitted

m11_s03_optional_columns_meaning.title: What makes a column optional?
m11_s03_optional_columns_meaning.prompt: What is the main idea of an optional column during inserts?

help.concept: An optional column is one that does not always need a direct supplied value.
help.hint_1: Think it can sometimes be left NULL or omitted.
help.hint_2: It is different from a required column that must be filled somehow.

m11_s03_optional_columns_meaning.hint: Think a column that does not always need a direct value from you.

options.a: It does not always need a direct supplied value
options.b: It can never appear in the table

m11_s03_insert_behavior_meaning.title: What controls insert behavior for missing values?
m11_s03_insert_behavior_meaning.prompt: What mainly controls how an insert behaves when some values are missing?

help.concept: The schema rules decide whether a column is required, optional, or has a default.
help.hint_2: The table definition explains what SQL is allowed to do.

m11_s03_insert_behavior_meaning.hint: Think required, optional, and default rules in the schema.

options.a: The schema rules for required, optional, and defaulted columns
options.c: Only the comments in the query
options.d: The color of the SQL editor

m11_s03_null_vs_default.title: What is the difference between NULL and a default?
m11_s03_null_vs_default.prompt: What is the main beginner difference between NULL and a defaulted value?

help.concept: NULL is an explicit missing value, while a default is an automatic fallback value provided by the schema.
help.hint_1: Think missing value versus automatic fallback.
help.hint_2: One is supplied as NULL, and the other is filled in by the schema when omitted.

m11_s03_null_vs_default.hint: Think explicit missing value versus automatic fallback.

options.a: NULL is an explicit missing value, while a default is an automatic fallback value
options.b: They always mean exactly the same thing

m11_s03_null_true.title: What is true about NULL in inserts?
m11_s03_null_true.prompt: Select all true statements about using NULL during inserts.

help.concept: NULL can be used for optional columns when the schema allows it.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about optional columns and stored missing values.

m11_s03_null_true.hint: Think explicit missing value in an allowed column.

options.a: NULL can represent a missing value
options.b: NULL can be used in an optional column when allowed
options.c: A stored row can show NULL in that column afterward
options.d: NULL means the whole insert always fails
options.e: NULL is different from a defaulted value

m11_s03_defaults_true.title: What is true about defaults?
m11_s03_defaults_true.prompt: Select all true statements about default values during inserts.

help.concept: Defaults let omitted columns receive schema-provided values automatically.
help.hint_1: Think omitted column gets fallback value.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about automatic fill behavior.

options.a: A default can fill a column automatically when it is omitted
options.b: Defaults are defined by the schema
options.c: Defaults can make some inserts shorter
options.d: Defaults mean columns no longer exist
options.e: Reading the row afterward can show what default value was used

m11_s03_optional_columns_true.title: What is true about optional columns?
m11_s03_optional_columns_true.prompt: Select all true statements about optional columns in inserts.

help.concept: Optional columns give the insert some flexibility when the schema allows missing direct values.
help.hint_2: Choose the statements about columns that do not always need a direct supplied value.

m11_s03_optional_columns_true.hint: Think columns that may be left NULL or omitted.

options.a: Optional columns can make inserts more flexible
options.b: An optional column may allow NULL
options.c: An optional column may sometimes be omitted from the insert
options.d: Optional columns must always be supplied first
options.e: Knowing which columns are optional helps avoid mistakes

m11_s03_null_defaults_workflow_true.title: What matches a good beginner workflow for NULL and default inserts?
m11_s03_null_defaults_workflow_true.prompt: Select all statements that match a good beginner workflow for inserts involving NULL and defaults.

help.concept: A good workflow is to identify which columns are required, which are optional, and which have defaults before writing the insert.
help.hint_2: Choose the steps that help decide whether to supply, omit, or use NULL for each column.

m11_s03_null_defaults_workflow_true.hint: Decide which columns must be filled and which can be omitted or left NULL.

options.c: Identify which columns can use defaults when omitted
options.d: Choose whether to supply, omit, or use NULL for each column
